utterances: Thank you very much, Michele, great to be here.
Now, for folks who don't have an iPad or a Facebook or a Twitter account, describe this Flipboard for us.
How do you explain this, say, to your mom or your Aunt Mildred?
My mom, every now and then, will clip out an article from a magazine, and she'll mail it to me.
And that is basically an analog example of what people have been referring to in the tech industry as social media - people sharing articles and news with other people.
And what we did is try to take it one step further.
We wanted to make this whole experience of sharing links with people much more graphical - almost like a magazine from the future.
Things change in it; the pages have sort of a plastic, flipping feel.

It's a different kind of a magazine, but yet a lot of the traditional hallmarks of what make great magazines and what makes print so good - typography and beautiful photography, and beautiful layouts.
Those principles are what we built Flipboard around.
How did you come up with this idea?
But, it does seem, when you look at the Olympics, that it's an Olympic so far for the states of failure.

We have Chad Hedrick, who failed today, and I don't want to ruin the suspense for any of your listeners, but failed, he will not equal Eric Heiden's five gold medals, as he wanted to, because he lost in the team pursuit today.
We have Bode Miller, failing.
We have Michelle Kwan, withdrawing.
And then you have these people who, yeah, like Johnny Weir and like Ted Ligety who are capturing some of the spotlight.
As we look at these, of course, we're watching this all on TV, from far away, skipping around to various outlets of NBC here in the States.
Is what we're seeing on TV, does it bear any resemblance to what you see there, in and around these various venues as a reporter?
Well, it was, when I covered my first Olympics it was a shock.
And the reason is that TV does a marvelous job of making these Games seem like they all happen in one place.
If you're in Sydney, they're all happening right there by Sydney Harbor.
If you're here, they're all happening in the snowy mountains.
And what the truth is, is that they're far flung venues, you are racing from venue to venue but they can't be as far, you know, three or four hours apart.
And the trick is to wake up in the morning and make the proper guess as to what the story is going to be.
The other little nuance is we don't know what you're seeing, because in the media center we don't get the NBC feed.
And that may be because NBC doesn't want us to see what you're seeing because it may not look like what we're seeing.
But we don't get NBC, we just get, sort of an Olympic feed, which is by and large just the video.
And so we don't know what the Olympics look like to you.
And, nor do you know what people are necessarily saying about this.
Those TVs have been described as magic TVs, since you actually get the sports and no commentary.
Yeah, well, the funniest, and I'll give you a for instance.
During the Opening Ceremonies, Bode Miller did not wear a hat.

Now, that didn't seem like a particularly big deal, he was also sort of straggling in late, but we don't know if NBC is going to make a big deal of Bode Miller not wearing a hat, the only American who didn't wear a hat, and straggling in late.
Once upon a time the United States basketball players got in trouble for not, you know, for wrapping themselves in the flag and, in an inappropriate fashion, or something.
So we don't really know what the controversies are going to be, because they're only the controversies that NBC sort of wants to create.
And so, one of the discussions over here is, well, how big is that going to be?
How big a deal is that going to be back in America?
And it's really a curious phenomenon, because these are, this has become a wholly TV event.
Geoff Calkins, thanks very much.
Thanks very much, Neal.
Geoff Calkins writes about sports for the Memphis Commercial Appeal.
You can read the latest Olympic results, see photos, and keep up with the medal standings at our website, npr.org.
I'm Neal Conan, NPR News, in Washington.
Shut Up and Sing, a new documentary about the Dixie Chicks, is, not surprisingly, stirring up controversy.
The Weinstein Company, which is releasing the film, has condemned NBC and the new CW network for declining to air an ad for the movie.
NPR's Kim Masters reports.

Shut Up and Sing deals with matters familiar even to many who aren't fans of the Dixie Chicks: the controversy that erupted in March 2003, when singer Natalie Maines said during a concert that she was ashamed Mr. Bush is from Texas.
The 30-second ad for the film, which opened yesterday in New York and Los Angeles, includes that quote, but begins with another from the president.
(Soundbite of movie ""Shut Up and Sing"")
President GEORGE W.
BUSH: The United States and our allies are authorized to use force in Iraq.
If you think we're living in a free society...
We're ashamed the president of the United States is from Texas.
(Soundbite of cheering)
Wait until you disagree with it.
They had the hottest song and it died.
The true story...
Unidentified Man #1: Move the fridge, Dixie Chicks.
...of a conspiracy...
On Thursday, the Weinstein Company, which is distributing the film, accused NBC and the CW of censoring the ads on political grounds.
Documents from both networks appear to support that claim.
A form from the NBC Advertising Standards Department bears a notation reading, We cannot accept these spots as they are disparaging to President Bush.
A similar form from the CW states more ambiguously that the network does not have appropriate programming in which to schedule the spot.
The CW airs such shows as Veronica Mars and America's Next Top Model.
In a statement, the CW said the Weinstein Company allegation is inaccurate, but declined to say whether it would in fact air the ad.
NBC said the spot violates its policy on ads that deal with issue of public controversy.
In response to a query from NPR, NBC acknowledged that it had previously aired spots for Fahrenheit 911, a film by Michael Moore that was deeply critical of the Bush administration.
The network did not explain the apparent inconsistency.
The television networks do have a long history of making content decisions based on economic and political sensitivities.
Kathryn Montgomery, a professor of communication at American University, says the networks have every right to choose what ads they will air.
But she notes that the broadcast networks also have major business before the federal government.
From a sort of principle point of view, you would hope that they wouldn't make politically motivated decisions here.
From a more kind of realistic perspective, it's not surprising that they do.
Andrew Schwartzman, of the Public Interest Media Access Project, agrees that the networks are within their rights, but he condemns such decisions.
Google had some pretty stellar results.
They had earnings of more than $1.6 billion, and that is the most money the company has ever made in a three-month period.
Google's chief executive, Eric Schmidt, says he thinks the worst of the recession is over.

Google is still a long way from the kind of double-digit growth it once enjoyed, but analysts believe its search advertising - which is the majority of the company's business - is going to bounce back faster than online display ads.
So, recovery is likely to be slower at companies like Yahoo and Time Warner's AOL, which rely heavily on display ads.
IBM sales actually fell last quarter but net income was up 14 percent.

Many companies are still spending less on technology, but IBM is increasing its revenue by relying more on outsourcing and other services that save clients money and are more profitable than selling hardware.
Since the mid-1990s, IBM has transformed itself from a hardware company on the brink of collapse to a one-stop technology shop.
Laura Sydell, NPR News, San Francisco.
Governor Perry's speech was just part of his effort to climb out of single digits in the polls.
He also notified Iowa television stations that he'll soon begin placing ads.
GOP candidates have debated.
They have given speeches and traipsed to the usual diners and coffee shops, but the ad wars have been relatively slow to start.
More on that from NPR's Mara Liasson.
This year, the Republican candidates have a different approach to the media.
One thing that's changed: television advertising is starting later than usual.
It's not as though they're not making ads.
It's just that, so far, we have not seen nearly as many of them on our TV sets.
That's Robert Thompson, professor of television and popular culture at Syracuse University.
If voters aren't seeing ads on TV, they can see them on their computers.
Web ads are easy to find.
Mostly, they're mash-ups of the candidates' comments from the debates, which have been the main event of the Republican race for the last two months.
Here's Rick Perry's ad attacking Mitt Romney with his own words.
(SOUNDBITE OF RICK PERRY'S CAMPAIGN AD)
And here's Romney's Web video attacking Perry.
(SOUNDBITE OF MITT ROMNEY'S CAMPAIGN AD)
These Web ads won't get a guaranteed number of viewers the way TV ads used to, but, Thompson says, they are useful for another reason.
It brings a lot of people, who are already supporters, to the site.
They watch this stuff.
Hopefully, they make contributions.
If you put something up that gets a lot of attention, eventually those things get picked up by CNN and Fox News and MSNBC.
And that's free media.

According to Andrew Rasiej, the founder of Personal Democracy Media - a website focused on how technology is changing politics - all advertising, along with outreach through social media like Facebook and Twitter, are being used by the candidates to build a community of supporters.
Romney has been at it longer than Perry, Rasiej says, but that can change.

Rick Perry may not be as effective as Romney at the moment, but, over time, he will be more and more effective because over several weeks, and as television ads start to hit the airwaves, it draws more people to the Web, more people to sign up, more people to talk to each other, and eventually a community is formed.

And those communities, built with the new tools of social media, are guaranteed to be much bigger than they were four years ago, when there were 1.5 billion views of online video that mentioned Obama or McCain in the title.
